2. Store Operations Terms
3. Supply Chain & Fulfillment Terms
4. Sales, Marketing & Merchandising Terms
5. Omnichannel & eCommerce Terms

Term: SKU
Full Form: Stock Keeping Unit
Explanation: Unique ID for each product variant
Example: Red, Medium Nike T-shirt
Why Needed: Granular product tracking
Used By: Inventory, Merchandising

Term: UPC
Full Form: Universal Product Code
Explanation: 12-digit barcode for scanning
Example: Barcode for Lay's chips
Why Needed: Standardized identification
Used By: POS, IT, Master Data

Term: Assortment
Full Form: --
Explanation: Variety of products in a category
Example: 6 coffee brands × 3 sizes
Why Needed: Enhances choice & planning
Used By: Merchandising, Category Managers

Term: Stock Turnover
Full Form: --
Explanation: Frequency of inventory sale & replacement
Example: 4 shampoo restocks/year
Why Needed: Indicates movement
Used By: Inventory, Finance

Term: On-Hand Inventory
Full Form: --
Explanation: Currently available stock
Example: 18 Dove shampoos in store
Why Needed: Aids restocking
Used By: WMS, Store Ops

Term: Safety Stock
Full Form: --
Explanation: Buffer stock for demand spikes
Example: 10 extra iPhones held
Why Needed: Avoids stockouts
Used By: Planning, Supply Chain

Term: Reorder Point
Full Form: --
Explanation: Level triggering auto reordering
Example: <20 toilet rolls = auto order
Why Needed: Ensures availability
Used By: Inventory System

Term: Cycle Count
Full Form: --
Explanation: Periodic partial stock count
Example: Weekly aisle count
Why Needed: Keeps records accurate
Used By: Store Ops, Inventory Control

Term: Dead Stock
Full Form: --
Explanation: Unsellable or obsolete stock
Example: Halloween candy in Dec
Why Needed: Requires clearance
Used By: Merchandising, Finance

Term: Inventory Turn
Full Form: --
Explanation: Annual inventory turnover rate
Example: 8 turns/year
Why Needed: Measures efficiency
Used By: Finance, Merchandising

Term: Fill Rate
Full Form: --
Explanation: % of demand met from inventory
Example: 95 fulfilled out of 100
Why Needed: Indicates service level
Used By: Supply Chain, Planning

Term: Stock-to-Sales Ratio
Full Form: --
Explanation: Stock level vs. sales volume
Example: ₹100K stock/₹20K sales
Why Needed: Identifies overstocking
Used By: Finance, Merchandising

Term: MOQ
Full Form: Minimum Order Quantity
Explanation: Smallest orderable quantity
Example: MOQ = 100 shirts
Why Needed: Ensures supplier efficiency
Used By: Procurement

Term: Backorder
Full Form: --
Explanation: Demand for out-of-stock items
Example: Console on backorder
Why Needed: Retains demand
Used By: Order Mgmt, Inventory

Term: Auto Replenishment
Full Form: --
Explanation: System-driven stock reorder
Example: Inventory < 30 → auto order
Why Needed: Saves manual work
Used By: Planning, IT

Term: Demand Forecasting
Full Form: --
Explanation: Predicts future sales using data
Example: 10K jackets for winter
Why Needed: Enables accurate planning
Used By: Planners, Buyers

Term: ATP
Full Form: Available to Promise
Explanation: Uncommitted available inventory
Example: 50 units ATP = 50 units shippable
Why Needed: Avoids false promises
Used By: Order Mgmt

Term: Assortment Rationalization
Full Form: --
Explanation: Streamlining SKUs to optimize
Example: Removing slow sellers
Why Needed: Improves profit/mix
Used By: Merchandising

Term: Expiry Management
Full Form: --
Explanation: Tracking products with expiry dates
Example: Dairy products with 7-day shelf life
Why Needed: Prevents waste
Used By: Inventory, WMS

Term: Overstock
Full Form: --
Explanation: Inventory beyond forecasted demand
Example: 1000 units instead of 600
Why Needed: Ties up capital
Used By: Inventory Planners

Term: POS
Full Form: Point of Sale
Explanation: Checkout system for transactions
Example: Scanning at cash counter
Why Needed: Updates billing & stock
Used By: Store Ops, IT

Term: Planogram
Full Form: --
Explanation: Shelf arrangement layout
Example: Toothpaste planogram
Why Needed: Improves display consistency
Used By: Visual Merchandising

Term: Shrinkage
Full Form: --
Explanation: Inventory loss from theft/errors
Example: 5 items missing
Why Needed: Impacts margins
Used By: Loss Prevention

Term: Facing
Full Form: --
Explanation: No. of products visible on shelf
Example: 4 Coke bottles front-facing
Why Needed: Attracts customer attention
Used By: Merchandising

Term: BOH / FOH
Full Form: Back / Front of House
Explanation: Stockroom vs. shop floor
Example: Stock BOH, sales FOH
Why Needed: Helps organization
Used By: Ops, Store Staff

Term: Stock Replenishment
Full Form: --
Explanation: Refilling shelves from stock
Example: Milk restocked on aisle
Why Needed: Maintains availability
Used By: Store Team

Term: Stock Cover
Full Form: --
Explanation: How long stock will last
Example: 4 weeks of shampoo
Why Needed: Guides purchase
Used By: Planning, Merchandising

Term: Mystery Shopping
Full Form: --
Explanation: Evaluation done via disguised customers
Example: Review of store service by hired shopper
Why Needed: Quality control & compliance
Used By: CX, Retail Operations

Term: Plan Compliance
Full Form: --
Explanation: Adherence to store setup plans
Example: Store A follows display plan 100%
Why Needed: Standardization across stores
Used By: Store Audit Team

Term: GRN
Full Form: Goods Receipt Note
Explanation: Confirms goods received
Example: 50 jeans GRN generated
Why Needed: Triggers payment & update
Used By: Warehouse, Finance

Term: ASN
Full Form: Advanced Shipping Notice
Explanation: Pre-arrival shipping info
Example: 200 laptops ASN sent
Why Needed: Preps receiving team
Used By: Vendor Mgmt, WH

Term: Putaway
Full Form: --
Explanation: Placing goods in storage
Example: Soaps to Bin A7
Why Needed: Space utilization
Used By: Warehouse Ops

Term: Picking
Full Form: --
Explanation: Selecting items to fulfill orders
Example: Shirt & jeans picked
Why Needed: Affects accuracy & speed
Used By: Fulfillment Team

Term: Cross-Docking
Full Form: --
Explanation: No storage; direct ship from inbound to outbound
Example: Shoes received and shipped
Why Needed: Cuts storage time
Used By: DC, Logistics

Term: Lead Time
Full Form: --
Explanation: Time between order & delivery
Example: 10 days for cereal
Why Needed: Affects planning
Used By: Procurement, Planning

Term: Slotting
Full Form: --
Explanation: Strategic item placement
Example: Milk near dispatch door
Why Needed: Enhances pick speed
Used By: WMS Admins

Term: Reverse Logistics
Full Form: --
Explanation: Handling returns
Example: Blender returned & sent to vendor
Why Needed: Customer satisfaction
Used By: Returns, Customer Care

Term: ATP
Full Form: Available to Promise
Explanation: Unallocated stock
Example: 100 ATP units = can sell 100
Why Needed: Real-time order commitment
Used By: Order Mgmt

Term: Pick-to-Light / Put-to-Light
Full Form: --
Explanation: Lights guide picking/putaway
Example: Light flashes at Bin B3
Why Needed: Reduces picking errors
Used By: Warehouse Ops

Term: DC
Full Form: Distribution Center
Explanation: Central storage/dispatch hub
Example: Orders ship from DC
Why Needed: Fast fulfillment
Used By: Logistics

Term: Omnichannel Order Routing
Full Form: --
Explanation: Assign order to optimal node
Example: Route order to nearest store
Why Needed: Reduces cost, speeds up
Used By: Order Mgmt

Term: Cross-Border Fulfillment
Full Form: --
Explanation: Shipping items to another country
Example: Shipping apparel from US to Canada
Why Needed: Expands market
Used By: Logistics, Compliance

Term: Bulk Picking
Full Form: --
Explanation: Picking multiple orders simultaneously
Example: 5 shirts picked for 3 orders
Why Needed: Improves efficiency
Used By: Warehouse Planning

Term: Markdown
Full Form: --
Explanation: Price cut for promotions or clearance
Example: ₹999 → ₹599
Why Needed: Clears inventory
Used By: Merchandising

Term: Sell-Through
Full Form: --
Explanation: % of stock sold in period
Example: 80% sold in 2 weeks
Why Needed: Assesses item performance
Used By: Buyers

Term: Lift
Full Form: --
Explanation: Sales boost due to promotion
Example: Chips sell more near soda
Why Needed: Marketing impact
Used By: Trade Team

Term: Basket Size
Full Form: --
Explanation: Avg. items or ₹ per transaction
Example: 5 items/customer
Why Needed: Shows engagement
Used By: Store Ops

Term: GMROI
Full Form: Gross Margin Return on Investment
Explanation: Profit per ₹1 of inventory
Example: ₹200 profit on ₹100 = 2.0 GMROI
Why Needed: Evaluates SKU efficiency
Used By: Merch, Finance

Term: Visual Merchandising
Full Form: --
Explanation: Displaying products to boost sales
Example: Wedding suits in front window
Why Needed: Attracts footfall
Used By: Visual Team

Term: Plan vs Actual
Full Form: --
Explanation: Planned vs. real performance
Example: Target ₹5L, achieved ₹4.8L
Why Needed: Course correction
Used By: Sales, Analytics

Term: Promotion Calendar
Full Form: --
Explanation: Scheduled promotional plan
Example: Diwali Sale in October
Why Needed: Drives campaigns
Used By: Marketing, Merchandising

Term: Conversion Rate
Full Form: --
Explanation: Visitors who purchase
Example: 100 visitors, 20 buyers = 20%
Why Needed: Measures effectiveness
Used By: eCom, Store Sales

Term: Omnichannel
Full Form: --
Explanation: Unified experience across all channels
Example: Browse app → buy store
Why Needed: Customer convenience
Used By: CX, eCom

Term: BOPIS
Full Form: Buy Online Pickup In-Store
Explanation: Online order → store pickup
Example: Charger pickup from MW store
Why Needed: Saves delivery time/cost
Used By: Ops, eCom

Term: ROPIS
Full Form: Reserve Online Pickup In-Store
Explanation: Online reserve → pay/pick later
Example: Dress reserved → tried in-store
Why Needed: Brings foot traffic
Used By: Store Ops

Term: Click & Collect
Full Form: --
Explanation: Same as BOPIS (Europe term)
Example: Grocery ordered online, collected
Why Needed: Convenience
Used By: Store + eCom

Term: Dropshipping
Full Form: --
Explanation: Vendor ships directly to customer
Example: Sunglasses from third party
Why Needed: Saves inventory cost
Used By: eCom, Vendor Mgmt

Term: Last Mile Delivery
Full Form: --
Explanation: Final delivery leg to customer
Example: Order sent by courier
Why Needed: Crucial for satisfaction
Used By: Logistics

Term: Endless Aisle
Full Form: --
Explanation: In-store access to online inventory
Example: Customer orders color not in-store
Why Needed: Increases conversion
Used By: Omnichannel, eCom

Term: Ship-from-Store
Full Form: --
Explanation: Orders shipped directly from stores
Example: Fulfilling online order from nearby store
Why Needed: Speeds delivery
Used By: Order Mgmt, Store Ops

Term: CRM
Full Form: Customer Relationship Mgmt
Explanation: Centralized customer data & comms
Example: Stores purchase history
Why Needed: Personalized offers
Used By: Marketing, IT

Term: CLV
Full Form: Customer Lifetime Value
Explanation: Total value of a customer
Example: ₹10K/year × 5 = ₹50K
Why Needed: Segments loyal customers
Used By: Analytics

Term: Churn Rate
Full Form: --
Explanation: % of customers lost over time
Example: 20% churn in loyalty program
Why Needed: Retention metric
Used By: Loyalty Team

Term: NPS
Full Form: Net Promoter Score
Explanation: Satisfaction measure (0-10 scale)
Example: Rated 9 → Promoter
Why Needed: Brand health indicator
Used By: CX Team

Term: Loyalty Program
Full Form: --
Explanation: Points/benefits for repeat purchases
Example: ₹1 = 1 pt; 1000 pts = ₹500
Why Needed: Builds loyalty
Used By: CRM, Marketing

Term: Segmentation
Full Form: --
Explanation: Grouping customers by behavior/value
Example: Budget vs. premium shoppers
Why Needed: Tailors marketing
Used By: Analytics, CRM

Term: Gross Margin
Full Form: --
Explanation: Sale price - COGS
Example: ₹500 - ₹300 = ₹200
Why Needed: Tracks profit
Used By: Finance

Term: Sell-In vs Sell-Out
Full Form: --
Explanation: Sell-In: to retailer; Sell-Out: to customer
Example: 100 shirts sold to store; 80 sold to customers
Why Needed: Tracks sell-through
Used By: Merchandising

Term: OTB
Full Form: Open to Buy
Explanation: Budget left for purchasing stock
Example: ₹5L left this quarter
Why Needed: Controls buying
Used By: Planning, Finance

Term: Cost-to-Serve
Full Form: --
Explanation: All costs to fulfill an order
Example: ₹300 to deliver to Ladakh
Why Needed: Price strategy input
Used By: Strategy, Finance

Term: Forecast Accuracy
Full Form: --
Explanation: How close forecasts match sales
Example: 90% accurate prediction
Why Needed: Helps avoid over/understock
Used By: Analytics

Term: Sales per Sq. Ft.
Full Form: --
Explanation: Sales from 1 sq. ft. of store space
Example: ₹8000/sq. ft.
Why Needed: Measures store performance
Used By: Retail Ops

Term: Markdown Optimization
Full Form: --
Explanation: Strategy to reduce price profitably
Example: ₹2,000 → ₹1,500 to boost sales
Why Needed: Preserves margin
Used By: Pricing, Analytics

Term: Inventory Valuation
Full Form: --
Explanation: Assigning financial value to stock
Example: ₹5L stock at year-end
Why Needed: Needed for accounting
Used By: Finance

Term: Breakeven Point
Full Form: --
Explanation: Revenue needed to cover all costs
Example: ₹1L fixed costs, ₹2K margin/product = 50 units
Why Needed: Determines profit threshold
Used By: Finance, Strategy

Introduction to Retail 101 - Core Process Flows

Hello, and welcome to this video-led learning program.
This program - Retail 101 - is an essential foundation for anyone working in the retail domain.
We'll be covering five core process flows that are key to understanding how retail works:

text: Retail 101 Training
Slide 1 - An Introduction to the Retail Sector
[Slide 1] Slide Content (shown on PPT):
[Slide 1] Retail Ecosystem & Value Chain - from manufacturers to customers
[Slide 1] Retail Formats - supermarkets, convenience stores, e-commerce, omnichannel
[Slide 1] Retail Processes - procurement, inventory, fulfillment, customer service
[Slide 1] Retail Systems - POS, ERP, OMS & supporting technologies
[Slide 1] Regulations & KPIs - compliance & performance measures
[Slide 1] Emerging Trends - digital tech, data-driven practices, evolving expectations

[Slide 1] Narration Script (to be spoken):
"Welcome to this Retail 101 program. This session will give you a solid foundation in how the retail sector works.
[Slide 1] We'll start by understanding the retail ecosystem and value chain - how goods and value flow from manufacturers all the way to customers.
[Slide 1] Next, we'll look at different retail formats, including supermarkets, convenience stores, e-commerce platforms, and omnichannel models that combine online and offline experiences.
[Slide 1] We'll then dive into the core retail processes like procurement, inventory management, order fulfillment, and customer service - all of which ensure smooth operations.
[Slide 1] Supporting these processes are critical retail systems such as POS, ERP, and OMS that help retailers run efficiently.
[Slide 1] We'll also touch upon regulations and KPIs - covering compliance requirements and how performance is measured.
[Slide 1] Finally, we'll explore emerging trends, such as the use of digital technologies, data-driven practices, and how retailers are adapting to changing customer expectations.
[Slide 1] This introduction sets the stage for the deeper dive into each area in the upcoming slides."

Slide 2 - Agenda - Retail 101 Training
Slide Content (shown on PPT):
[Slide 2] Module 1: Introduction to Retail
[Slide 2] Module 2: Key Retail Terminologies
[Slide 2] Module 3: Core Retail Concepts
[Slide 2] Module 4: Retail Operations
[Slide 2] Module 5: Governance & Performance
[Slide 2] Module 6: Retail Landscape & Future

[Slide 2] Module 7: Wrap-up & Knowledge Check

Narration

[Slide 2] "Here's the agenda for our Retail 101 training.
We'll begin with Module 1, where we introduce the retail ecosystem, its importance, the evolution of retail, and the different retail formats.
Module 2 will familiarize you with the most common retail terminologies and acronyms like POS, SKU, and ERP, helping you understand how they're used in daily operations.
In Module 3, we'll dive into core retail concepts, including the retail value chain, customer journey, and assortment management.
Module 4 is about retail operations - we'll cover supply chain basics, key retail systems like POS, OMS, WMS, ERP, and CRM, and also look at omnichannel retail, payments, private labels, and loss prevention.
Module 5 focuses on governance and performance, where we'll discuss compliance requirements and the KPIs used to measure retail success.
Module 6 will take a broader view of the retail landscape - looking at global and Indian leaders, the role of technology, and emerging trends shaping the future of retail.
Finally, in Module 7, we'll wrap up the program with a summary of key learnings, and conduct a short knowledge check or Q&A to reinforce your understanding."

Slide 3 - PPT Content (on slide)

[Slide 2] Welcome to Module 1: Introduction to Retail
This module covers:
[Slide 2] Retail Ecosystem - Overview
[Slide 2] Importance of Retail
[Slide 2] Evolution of Retail

[Slide 2] Types of Retail Formats

Narration

[Slide 2] "Welcome to Module 1: Introduction to Retail.
This module is designed to give you the foundation you need before we move into detailed retail processes.
[Slide 2] We'll be covering four key areas: the retail ecosystem, the importance of retail, its evolution over time, and different retail formats.

[Slide 2] Before we dive deeper, let's start with the Retail Ecosystem. Think of it as the big picture -- all the players and functions working together to deliver a product or service to the end consumer.
[Slide 2] In the next slide, we'll explore how this ecosystem flows step by step, from the manufacturer all the way to the customer."
Slide 4 - PPT Content (on slide)
[Slide 4] Retail Ecosystem: Overview
[Slide 4] Retail = selling goods/services directly to consumers (final stage of supply chain).
[Slide 4] Flow: Manufacturer → Wholesaler/Distributor → Retailer → Customer.
[Slide 4] Key Enablers: Logistics | Finance | IT (POS/ERP) | Marketing.
[Slide 4] Retail is customer-facing - trust, convenience, and brand perception depend on it.
[Slide 4] Example: Nike - manufactured in Vietnam → shipped to warehouses → sold via Nike stores/online → customer purchase defines the retail moment.
[Slide 4] Narration (to be spoken)
[Slide 4] "Retail is the process of selling goods and services directly to end consumers. It represents the final stage of the supply chain -- where products finally reach the people who use them.
[Slide 4] The flow is simple: manufacturers create products, which then move to wholesalers or distributors, then to retailers, and finally to customers.

[Slide 4] Supporting this flow are key enablers such as logistics for storage and transport, finance for payments and credit, IT systems like POS and ERP for smooth operations, and marketing to drive demand.

[Slide 4] What makes retail unique is that it is customer-facing. Retailers are not just moving goods; they are building trust and convenience at the last mile. A poor retail experience can damage the perception of the entire brand.

[Slide 4] Take Nike, for example. Shoes may be manufactured in Vietnam, shipped to Nike warehouses, and then sold through Nike's own stores or online platforms. The customer's final buying experience -- whether in-store or online -- is what defines retail.

[Slide 4] In essence, retail is the bridge between the world of production and the world of consumption."

Slide 5 - Importance of Retail
[Slide 4] Key Points:
[Slide 4] Economic Growth - Major GDP contributor (India: ~10%, US: ~20%).
[Slide 4] Employment - 2nd largest employer in India after agriculture.
[Slide 4] Customer Value - Offers choice, convenience, and competitive pricing.
[Slide 4] Innovation Hub - Drives change (e.g., Amazon Go, Zepto's 10-min delivery).

[Slide 4] Narration 
"Retail is much more than just selling products. It's a powerful engine of the economy, contributing around 10% of India's GDP and nearly 20% in the US. It also creates millions of jobs, making it the second-largest employer in India after agriculture. For customers, retail ensures choice, convenience, and competitive pricing across multiple formats - whether offline, online, or through quick commerce. And beyond that, retail is constantly innovating. New models like Amazon Go's cashier-less stores or Zepto's 10-minute delivery are redefining consumer expectations every day."

Slide 6 - Evolution of Retail (1/3): Traditional Retail
[Slide 4] PPT Content:
[Slide 4] Dominated by kirana & mom-and-pop stores.
[Slide 4] Built on trust, relationships, and community.
[Slide 4] Customers often purchased on credit.
[Slide 4] Personalized shopping experience.
[Slide 4] Focus on convenience & reliability, not discounts.
[Slide 4] Example: Local kirana delivering groceries & maintaining handwritten dues.

[Slide 4] Narration:
"In the early stages, retail in India and across the world was dominated by small kirana shops and mom-and-pop stores. These stores were deeply community-driven, built on trust and personal relationships. Customers often purchased items on credit, with payments settled later. The experience was highly personalized, as shopkeepers usually knew the family's preferences. The focus was not variety or discounts, but rather convenience and reliability. A good example is your neighborhood kirana store delivering groceries at home and keeping a handwritten record of dues."
Slide 7 - Evolution of Retail (2/3): Organized Retail
[Slide 7] PPT Content:
[Slide 7] Emerged with urbanization & economic growth.
[Slide 7] Department Stores (1800s): Multiple categories, destination shopping.
[Slide 7] Supermarkets/Hypermarkets (1900s): Self-service, bulk buying, discount pricing.
[Slide 7] Discount Stores: Low prices, high volumes, value-conscious customers.
[Slide 7] Examples: Walmart (global), Big Bazaar & DMart (India).

[Slide 7] Narration:
"With urbanization and economic development, retail formats became more structured and organized. Department stores in the 1800s offered multiple categories under one roof, making them a one-stop destination. In the 1900s, supermarkets and hypermarkets introduced self-service, bulk buying, and discount pricing, turning shopping into an experience. Discount stores also emerged, focusing on affordability and attracting value-conscious shoppers. For example, Walmart became a global leader in supply chain efficiency, while in India, Big Bazaar popularized hypermarkets and DMart built its success on everyday low pricing."
Slide 8 - Evolution of Retail (3/3): Digital & Future Retail
[Slide 8] PPT Content:
[Slide 8] Technology shifted retail to digital-first.
[Slide 8] E-commerce (2000s): Amazon, Flipkart, Myntra - shopping anytime, anywhere.
[Slide 8] Omnichannel (2010s): Integrated online & offline journey.
[Slide 8] Future Retail: AI personalization, AR/VR shopping, robotics, data-driven insights.
[Slide 8] Example: AR try-on features, AI chatbots, Amazon robotics.

[Slide 8] Narration:
"The next big transformation came with technology, moving retail from physical-first to digital-first. In the 2000s, e-commerce platforms like Amazon, Flipkart, and Myntra allowed customers to shop anytime, anywhere, breaking geographical barriers. The 2010s brought omnichannel retail, integrating offline and online journeys -- for example, browsing online but purchasing in-store or choosing click-and-collect options. Looking ahead, future retail will be shaped by AI-driven personalization, AR and VR shopping experiences, robotics in warehouses, and advanced data analytics. Examples include AR try-on features in fashion apps, AI chatbots for personalized recommendations, and Amazon's robotic warehouses."
Slide 9 - Types of Retail Formats (1/2)
[Slide 9] PPT Content:
[Slide 9] Department Stores: Multiple categories under one roof.
[Slide 9] Supermarkets: Groceries & essentials, self-service, competitive pricing.
[Slide 9] Hypermarkets: Larger scale, groceries + clothing + electronics, bulk sales & discounts.
[Slide 9] Examples: Shoppers Stop (India), Walmart (US), Carrefour (global), Big Bazaar & DMart (India).

[Slide 9] Narration:
"Now let's look at the different types of retail formats. Department stores offer multiple categories under one roof -- from clothing and cosmetics to footwear and home furnishings. A good example is Shoppers Stop in India. Supermarkets focus mainly on groceries and daily essentials, usually in a self-service format with attractive pricing -- think Walmart in the US or Big Bazaar in India. Hypermarkets take this a step further, operating on a much larger scale. They combine groceries, clothing, electronics, and even appliances, with an emphasis on bulk sales and big discounts. Examples include Carrefour globally and DMart in India."
Slide 10 - Types of Retail Formats (2/2)
[Slide 10] Content (for PPT):
[Slide 10] Discount Stores → Low prices, high volumes (e.g., Dollar Tree, DMart)
[Slide 10] Specialty Stores → Focus on single category, expertise & variety (e.g., Nike, Sephora)
[Slide 10] Convenience Stores → Small format, quick buys near homes/offices (e.g., 7-Eleven)
[Slide 10] E-commerce → Online shopping, price comparison, home delivery (e.g., Amazon, Flipkart)
[Slide 10] Omnichannel → Seamless online + offline journey (e.g., Target, Reliance Digital)

[Slide 10] Narration (for delivery):
"Moving forward, let's explore more formats.

[Slide 10] We begin with Discount Stores, which are built around the idea of low prices and high volumes, making them highly attractive to price-sensitive customers. Think of Dollar Tree in the US or DMart in India.

[Slide 10] Next are Specialty Stores. These focus on a single product category and build deep expertise, offering wide variety and enhanced customer experiences. Examples include Nike for sportswear or Sephora for beauty.
[Slide 10] Then we have Convenience Stores. These are small outlets designed for quick purchases, often strategically located near homes or workplaces. 7-Eleven is a global leader in this space.
[Slide 10] Of course, E-commerce has revolutionized retail. Online platforms like Amazon and Flipkart make it easy to shop from anywhere, compare prices, and get doorstep delivery.

[Slide 10] And finally, we have Omnichannel Retail, which is the latest trend. It combines offline and online seamlessly - so customers can check availability online, try in-store, and even get home delivery. Retailers like Target in the US and Reliance Digital in India are actively adopting this model."

Slide 11 - 
Module 2: Key Retail Terminologies
[Slide 10] Content (for PPT):
[Slide 10] Retail has its own vocabulary
[Slide 10] Used by managers, buyers, supply chain, and tech teams
[Slide 10] Learn common acronyms & terms: POS, SKU, ERP, CRM, etc.

[Slide 10] Narration (for delivery):
"Welcome to Module 2: Key Retail Terminologies. Just like every profession has its own language, retail too has a vocabulary that's widely used by store managers, buyers, supply chain teams, and even technology partners. In this module, we'll get familiar with the most important retail terms and acronyms--like POS, SKU, ERP, CRM, and many more--that you'll come across in day-to-day retail operations."
Slide 12 - Store & Product Related Terms
[Slide 12] Content (for PPT):
[Slide 12] SKU (Stock Keeping Unit): Unique ID per product variant
[Slide 12] UPC / Barcode: Standardized code for scanning & billing
[Slide 12] Category Management: Grouping products to improve sales
[Slide 12] Planogram (POG): Guide for store product placement
[Slide 12] Private Label: Retailer-owned brands
[Slide 12] Assortment: Range of products offered
[Slide 12] Facing: Number of product units visible on shelf

[Slide 12] Narration (for delivery):
"Let's begin with some store and product-related terms.
First, SKU or Stock Keeping Unit - this is a unique ID for each product variant, like a blue shirt in size M.
Next, UPC or Barcode - this is the standardized code you see on products, used for scanning and billing at the checkout.
Then, Category Management - the practice of logically grouping products to optimize sales and make shopping easier for customers.
We also have the Planogram, or POG, which is essentially a store layout guide showing exactly where products should be placed on shelves.
Another key concept is Private Label, which refers to retailer-owned brands--for example, Amazon Basics or DMart Ready.
Assortment refers to the range of products that a store or online platform offers.
And finally, Facing is the number of product units visible to customers on the shelf--it's about presentation and visibility."
Slide 13 - Sales & Checkout Terms
[Slide 13] Content (for PPT) →
[Slide 13] POS (Point of Sale): Billing system (cash registers, digital POS).
[Slide 13] Basket Size: Average number of items per transaction.
[Slide 13] Footfall: Number of customers visiting a store.
[Slide 13] Conversion Rate: % of visitors who actually purchase.
[Slide 13] Shrinkage: Inventory loss due to theft, damage, or errors.
[Slide 13] AOV (Average Order Value): Average spend per customer order.
[Slide 13] Cross-selling / Upselling: Suggesting additional or premium products at checkout.

[Slide 13] Narration (for delivery) 

Now, let's move into some sales and checkout terms that retailers use every day:
[Slide 13] POS, or Point of Sale, refers to the system used for billing--this could be a traditional cash register, or a digital POS that supports card payments, UPI, or even mobile wallets.
[Slide 13] Basket Size shows how many items, on average, a customer buys in a single transaction. A higher basket size usually means better sales per visit.
[Slide 13] Footfall is simply the count of people walking into a store. But what really matters is how many of them actually buy something--this is tracked by the Conversion Rate.
[Slide 13] Shrinkage is a challenge for retailers--it means loss of inventory because of theft, damage, or clerical errors.
[Slide 13] AOV, or Average Order Value, tracks the average amount spent per transaction. For example, if a store has 100 orders worth $5,000 in total, the AOV is $50.

[Slide 13] Finally, cross-selling and upselling are sales techniques at checkout. Cross-selling is about recommending a related product--like offering fries with a burger--while upselling is about suggesting a premium option, like a bigger size or higher-end model.

Slide 14 - Supply Chain & Inventory Terms
[Slide 13] Content (for PPT):
[Slide 13] ERP: Integrated system for operations.
[Slide 13] RFQ: Requesting supplier price quotes.
[Slide 13] PO: Official order sent to supplier.
[Slide 13] GRN: Record of goods received.
[Slide 13] WMS: Manages warehouse storage & movement.
[Slide 13] OMS: Manages customer orders across channels.
[Slide 13] FIFO: Oldest stock sold first.
[Slide 13] Lead Time: Time from order to receipt.

[Slide 13] Narration (for delivery):
"In retail, supply chain and inventory terms are crucial for smooth operations.
ERP, or Enterprise Resource Planning, is the integrated backbone system that connects procurement, finance, inventory, and sales.
RFQ is a request for suppliers to share their price quotes, while a PO or Purchase Order is the formal document that confirms the purchase.
When goods arrive, they are documented through a GRN, or Goods Receipt Note.
Inside the warehouse, a WMS, or Warehouse Management System, keeps track of how items are stored, moved, and dispatched.
The OMS, or Order Management System, handles customer orders across online and offline channels.
FIFO, First In First Out, ensures older stock is sold before new stock, reducing waste.
And finally, Lead Time measures the time taken between placing an order and actually receiving it -- a key factor for planning stock levels."
Slide 15 - Marketing & Customer Terms
[Slide 15] Content (for PPT):
[Slide 15] CRM: Tracks customer interactions.
[Slide 15] Loyalty Programs: Rewards for retention.
[Slide 15] Merchandising: Displays, offers, pricing.
[Slide 15] Promotion Calendar: Scheduled discounts & campaigns.
[Slide 15] CLV: Total long-term customer value.
[Slide 15] Market Basket Analysis: Products often bought together.
[Slide 15] Churn Rate: % of customers lost.

[Slide 15] Narration (for delivery):
"Retail success heavily depends on customer-focused strategies.
CRM, or Customer Relationship Management, helps retailers track interactions and history with each customer.
Loyalty programs such as reward points or cashback encourage customers to keep coming back.
Merchandising focuses on how products are displayed, priced, and promoted inside the store.
A promotion calendar is a planned schedule of discounts, festive offers, and campaigns.
Customer Lifetime Value, or CLV, estimates how much revenue a single customer contributes over their entire relationship with the retailer.
Market Basket Analysis helps identify products that are frequently purchased together -- for example, chips and soft drinks.

The retail customer journey is much more than a simple purchase--it's the entire experience a shopper has with a brand.
It starts with Awareness, when a customer first comes across a product through ads, social media, or even word-of-mouth.
Next is Consideration, where the shopper actively compares prices, reads reviews, and weighs alternatives. Retailers can influence this by offering promotions or personalized suggestions.
Then comes Purchase, where the customer finally buys--whether online or in-store. A hassle-free checkout, multiple payment methods, and smooth delivery make a huge difference here.
After the purchase, the Post-Purchase stage kicks in--covering returns, delivery, feedback, or customer service. Doing this well builds trust.
Finally, Loyalty is when the customer not only returns but also becomes an advocate for the brand. Loyalty programs and personalized rewards keep them engaged.
For example, a Zara shopper may see an ad online, browse the app, buy in-store, later return an item, and still stay loyal because of Zara's points program.

Slide 28 - Retail Supply Chain Basics

The retail supply chain is the backbone of operations. It ensures products move efficiently from suppliers to customers, balancing speed, cost, and availability.

[Slide 28] First, the core flow: goods usually travel from suppliers, to distribution centers, then to warehouses or stores, and finally to the customer. This structure balances bulk handling with local availability.
[Slide 28] Next, drop shipping - here, the supplier ships directly to the customer. It saves inventory costs for the retailer, though it may result in longer delivery timelines.
[Slide 28] Another model is cross-docking, where goods skip warehousing altogether and move straight from inbound trucks to outbound trucks. This reduces storage costs and speeds up delivery.

[Slide 28] Finally, the most expensive and customer-visible part - last-mile delivery. This is where products move from a store or hub to the customer's doorstep. Retailers invest heavily here, because delivery speed and accuracy directly shape customer satisfaction.
[Slide 28] For instance, Flipkart uses warehouses for fast-moving goods but also drop ships low-demand SKUs, while BigBasket focuses on last-mile efficiency with its own delivery fleet.

Private labels are in-house brands that retailers create and sell in their own stores. Instead of relying only on national brands, retailers source or manufacture products under their own label.

Slide 33 - Loss Prevention & Shrinkage in Retail
[Slide 33] Content (for PPT) →
[Slide 33] Shrinkage = gap between recorded vs actual inventory
[Slide 33] Causes:
[Slide 33] Shoplifting/theft (customers, employees)
[Slide 33] Fraud (fake returns, billing manipulation)
[Slide 33] Damage/spoilage (esp. food retail)
[Slide 33] Admin errors (wrong entry, miscounts)
[Slide 33] Avg shrinkage = 1.5-2% of sales → major loss
[Slide 33] Prevention methods:

[Slide 33] RFID & barcode tracking
[Slide 33] Regular audits & inventory checks
[Slide 33] AI & analytics → detect unusual behavior

[Slide 33] Narration (for delivery) →
Shrinkage in retail refers to the difference between what's recorded in inventory and what's actually available. This is a critical issue because retail already works on thin margins, and shrinkage averages 1.5-2% of total sales worldwide.

[Slide 33] Shrinkage happens due to multiple reasons - shoplifting or theft, both by customers and sometimes employees; fraudulent activities like fake returns or billing manipulation; damage and spoilage, especially in food retail; and simple administrative errors like wrong data entry or miscounts.
[Slide 33] To prevent these losses, retailers use several strategies:
[Slide 33] CCTV and security staff to deter theft.
[Slide 33] RFID and barcode systems to track product movement.
[Slide 33] Regular audits and inventory checks to catch discrepancies early.
[Slide 33] And now increasingly, AI and analytics to spot unusual patterns like suspicious returns or self-checkout theft.
[Slide 33] For example, Walmart uses AI-enabled cameras at self-checkout counters to detect when customers skip scanning items - significantly reducing theft.
[Slide 33] In summary, effective loss prevention not only saves costs but also protects profitability.

[Slide 36] Narration (for delivery) → 
"When we talk about measuring sales in retail, there are some standard KPIs that every retailer keeps a close eye on.
[Slide 36] First, Average Basket Value or ABV tells us the average money spent by a customer in one transaction. A higher ABV often means good upselling or cross-selling.
[Slide 36] Next, Same-Store Sales Growth is about measuring year-over-year growth for existing stores only. This helps us check whether growth is real or just coming from opening new outlets.
[Slide 36] Then we have the Conversion Rate, which is the percentage of visitors who actually make a purchase. This is super critical both in physical stores and online.
[Slide 36] Revenue per Square Foot is another key metric. It tells us how effectively we are using our store space to generate sales.
[Slide 36] Retailers also look at Gross Sales vs. Net Sales. Gross is total sales, but after discounts, returns, or cancellations, we get Net Sales - which is the real revenue.
[Slide 36] Sales per Employee measures productivity - how much revenue each staff member is helping to generate.

[Slide 36] Finally, Channel-wise Sales Mix gives us insight into how customers are shopping - whether they buy from stores, online websites, or apps. This is especially useful in today's omnichannel world.

[Slide 36] Together, these KPIs give a complete picture of how well sales are performing and where improvements can be made."

Slide 37 - Retail KPIs: Inventory
[Slide 36] Content (for PPT):
[Slide 36] Inventory Turnover Ratio - Speed at which stock is sold & replaced
[Slide 36] Days of Inventory (DOI) - Avg number of days stock sits before being sold
[Slide 36] Sell-through % - % of stock sold vs. stock received in a period
[Slide 36] Stockout Rate - % of times products are unavailable when demanded
[Slide 36] Backorder Rate - % of orders delayed due to inventory shortage
[Slide 36] GMROI - Profit earned for every ₹/$ invested in inventory
[Slide 36] Shrinkage % - Inventory lost due to theft, damage, or errors

[Slide 36] Narration (for delivery):
Now, let's focus on inventory KPIs, which help retailers manage stock efficiently and meet customer demand.
[Slide 36] First, Inventory Turnover Ratio tells us how fast stock is sold and replenished. Higher turnover means products move quickly, while lower turnover could signal overstocking.
[Slide 36] Next, Days of Inventory (DOI) measures how long items stay in inventory before being sold. If this number is high, it means stock is sitting too long, which can tie up capital.
[Slide 36] Sell-through percentage compares stock sold to stock received in a period. It helps retailers understand demand and plan inventory better.
[Slide 36] Stockout Rate shows how often products are unavailable when customers want them. High stockouts can lead to lost sales and unhappy customers.
[Slide 36] Backorder Rate measures orders delayed because stock wasn't available. It reflects supply chain efficiency and planning.
[Slide 36] GMROI, or Gross Margin Return on Inventory, tells us the profit earned for every rupee or dollar invested in inventory. This helps retailers evaluate the profitability of their stock.
[Slide 36] Finally, Shrinkage percentage highlights losses from theft, damage, or errors. Controlling shrinkage is important to protect profits.
[Slide 36] By understanding these KPIs, retailers can balance having enough stock to satisfy customers while keeping inventory costs under control.

[Slide 38] Narration (for delivery):
Next, let's talk about customer KPIs, which measure how happy and loyal customers are, and how effective the retailer is at serving them.
[Slide 38] Net Promoter Score (NPS) tells us how likely customers are to recommend a brand to others. A high NPS indicates strong customer loyalty and advocacy.
[Slide 38] Customer Satisfaction Score (CSAT) gathers direct feedback on the purchase or service experience. It's a simple measure of how pleased customers are with their interactions.
[Slide 38] Customer Retention Rate tracks the percentage of customers who continue to buy over time. Retaining customers is usually more cost-effective than acquiring new ones.

[Slide 38] Customer Lifetime Value (CLV) estimates the total revenue a customer generates over their relationship with the retailer. This helps prioritize high-value customers and plan loyalty programs.
[Slide 38] Repeat Purchase Rate shows the percentage of customers making multiple purchases. It indicates satisfaction and the effectiveness of marketing and engagement strategies.
[Slide 38] Finally, Average Resolution Time measures how quickly complaints or issues are resolved. Faster resolution improves customer satisfaction and strengthens trust in the brand.
[Slide 38] By monitoring these KPIs, retailers can focus on building strong customer relationships, boosting loyalty, and driving long-term growth.

[Slide 39] On-Time Delivery % measures the proportion of orders delivered on time as promised. High percentages indicate reliability and operational efficiency.

Retail Domain FAQs
A. Retail Fundamentals

Q: What is retail, and how is it different from wholesale?
A: Retail involves selling goods directly to consumers in small quantities, while wholesale refers to bulk selling to other businesses.

Q: What are the major retail formats?
A: Department stores, supermarkets, specialty stores, ecommerce platforms, factory outlets, convenience stores, and pop-ups.

Q: What is an omnichannel retail strategy?
A: An approach that offers customers a unified experience across channels -- store, website, mobile app, and customer service.

Q: What is a product lifecycle in retail?
A: The progression of a product through stages: Introduction, Growth, Maturity, and Decline -- affecting pricing and inventory decisions.

Q: What is seasonality in retail, and how does it affect planning?
A: Certain periods (festivals, weather changes) see sales spikes. Retailers adjust inventory, staffing, and promotions accordingly.

Q: What is a customer journey in retail?
A: It's the full experience a customer has with a brand -- from discovery and purchase to post-sale service and repeat engagement.
B. Retail Operations & Order Fulfillment

Q: What is the order lifecycle in retail?
A: Order placement → confirmation → picking → packing → shipping → delivery → possible return/refund.

Q: What are BOPIS, ROPIS, and dropshipping?
A: BOPIS: Buy Online, Pick Up In Store
ROPIS: Reserve Online, Pick Up In Store
Dropshipping: Supplier ships directly to customer, bypassing retailer's inventory.

Q: What is the role of the POS system in a retail store?
A: Processes billing, inventory updates, discounts, loyalty tracking, and real-time sales capture at the store.

Q: What is WMS (Warehouse Management System)?
A: It manages inventory flow in the warehouse -- including receiving, slotting, picking, packing, and shipping.

Q: What is reverse logistics?
A: The process of returning goods from the customer back to the retailer or supplier -- involving inspection, restocking, or refund.

Q: What does the 'last mile' in delivery mean?
A: The final leg of delivery -- from the distribution hub or store to the customer's doorstep. It's key to customer satisfaction.
C. Merchandising, Inventory & Supply Chain

Q: What is a SKU and why is it important?
A: A Stock Keeping Unit is a unique code assigned to each product variant -- vital for inventory tracking and reporting.

Q: What is assortment planning?
A: Strategic selection of products to stock in a store based on location, demand, season, and customer preferences.

Q: What is the difference between ATP, on-hand inventory, and safety stock?
A: ATP: Available to Promise -- sellable stock after accounting for committed stock
On-Hand: Physical quantity available
Safety Stock: Extra inventory held to prevent stockouts

Q: What is inventory turnover, and why does it matter?
A: It shows how often stock is sold and replenished. A higher rate indicates better inventory efficiency.

Q: What is a planogram?
A: A visual guide for how products should be placed on shelves to maximize visibility and sales consistency across stores.

Q: What is lead time in procurement?
A: The time between placing a purchase order and receiving the goods. Shorter lead times improve responsiveness.
D. Retail KPIs & Performance Metrics

Q: What are the top retail KPIs to track?
A: GMROI, Average Order Value (AOV), Conversion Rate, Sales per Sq. Ft., Forecast Accuracy, Churn Rate, and Sell-Through Rate.

Q: What is GMROI and why is it important?
A: Gross Margin Return on Investment indicates how much profit a retailer earns for every ₹1 invested in inventory.

Q: What's the difference between sell-in and sell-out?
A: Sell-In: Sale from brand to retailer
Sell-Out: Sale from retailer to end customer

Q: What is markdown optimization?
A: Data-driven strategy to reduce prices at the right time -- clearing stock while maintaining margins.

Q: How is promotion effectiveness measured?
A: Using metrics like lift in sales, redemption rates, profit margins, and customer conversion during campaigns.

Q: What is forecast accuracy, and why is it critical?
A: It measures how well projected demand matches actual sales -- ensuring the right inventory at the right time.
E. Systems, Roles & Domain Understanding

Q: What are the key systems used in retail tech stacks?
A: POS (Point of Sale), OMS (Order Management), WMS (Warehouse Management), ERP (Enterprise Resource Planning), CRM (Customer Relationship Management).

Q: What roles are involved in a retail transformation project?
A: Business Analysts, Developers, Testers, Product Owners, Visual Merchandisers, Store Operations, and Inventory Planners.

Q: Why is QA critical in retail applications?
A: Even small bugs in pricing, stock display, or checkout can result in revenue loss or customer dissatisfaction.

Q: What is the role of a merchandiser in retail?
A: To decide what products to sell, when, where, and at what price -- ensuring availability and alignment with customer demand.

Q: What is the importance of system integration in retail?
A: Integration ensures data flow across POS, OMS, ERP, CRM -- preventing mismatches in pricing, stock, and orders.

Q: Why is domain knowledge important for BAs, QAs, and Devs in retail?
A: It helps them build smarter, user-centric solutions and test real-world flows accurately (e.g., returns, promotions, inventory updates).

What Went Wrong: Tracking details were not pushed to the frontend due to API failure or delay in courier integration.
Who Was Involved: Customer, OMS, Courier Partner, Notification Engine, BA, Dev
Expected vs Actual: Expected: Order tracking should update in real-time
Actual: No visibility on order status
Impact: High support calls, negative feedback, anxious customer

metadata.title: Customer Can't Track Their Order

text:
Scenario 1: Order Not Routed to Nearest Store
Domain: OMS - Order Routing
Scenario: Customer from Mumbai places an online order. Instead of routing the order to the nearby Mumbai store, OMS routes it to a warehouse in Delhi.
What Went Wrong: Order routing logic didn't prioritize geo-location or nearest inventory.
Who Was Involved: Customer, OMS, Inventory System, Fulfillment Partner, BA, Dev
Expected vs Actual: Expected: Order should be routed to closest fulfillment location
Actual: Order sent from a faraway warehouse, increasing delivery time
Impact: Delayed delivery, higher logistics cost, poor customer experience

metadata.title: Order Not Routed to Nearest Store

text:
Scenario 2: Customer Unable to Cancel an Order
Domain: OMS - Order Modification
Scenario: A customer tries to cancel their order within 30 minutes of placing it, but the site shows "Order already shipped."
What Went Wrong: OMS did not respect the cancellation window and passed the order to fulfillment too early.
Who Was Involved: Customer, OMS, Website, Warehouse, BA, QA
Expected vs Actual: Expected: Cancellation should be allowed within window
Actual: Order shipped instantly, making cancellation impossible
Impact: Customer dissatisfaction, forced returns, waste of shipping cost

metadata.title: Customer Unable to Cancel an Order

text:
Scenario 3: Duplicate Order Created
Domain: OMS - Order Duplication
Scenario: Customer clicks "Place Order" once, but due to network lag, the system creates two identical orders and charges twice.
What Went Wrong: OMS didn't block duplicate order creation caused by repeated clicks.
Who Was Involved: Customer, OMS, Website, Payment Gateway, BA, QA
Expected vs Actual: Expected: One order and one payment should be created
Actual: Two orders placed, two payments deducted
Impact: Refund required, customer panic, support call generated

text:
Scenario 4: Partial Shipment Not Communicated
Domain: OMS - Fulfillment Updates
Scenario: Customer ordered 3 items. Only 2 were shipped, but the tracking page still shows "Order shipped" without details.
What Went Wrong: OMS didn't push partial shipment status clearly to the front end.
Who Was Involved: Customer, OMS, Frontend, Warehouse, Notification Engine
Expected vs Actual: Expected: System should show partial delivery and pending item(s)
Actual: Customer sees full order marked as shipped
Impact: Customer confusion, support queries, poor transparency

metadata.title: Partial Shipment Not Communicated

text:
Scenario 5: Refund Not Triggered After Return
Domain: OMS - Returns & Refunds
Scenario: Customer returns an item in-store and receives confirmation, but the refund is not credited even after 7 days.
What Went Wrong: Return status updated in POS, but OMS failed to trigger the refund workflow.
Who Was Involved: Customer, Store Associate, POS, OMS, Finance System, BA, QA
Expected vs Actual: Expected: Refund process should start automatically after return
Actual: Refund stuck due to missing event trigger in OMS
Impact: Customer frustration, support escalation, risk of brand damage

metadata.title: Refund Not Triggered After Return

text:
Scenario 1: Stock Shows Available but Item is Not Found in Store
Domain: Inventory Accuracy
Scenario: Customer places an order for pickup at a store where the system shows the item is in stock. However, when the associate checks, the item is missing.
What Went Wrong: Inventory was not updated after a damaged or misplaced item was removed physically but still reflected in the system.
Who Was Involved: Customer, Store Associate, Inventory System, OMS, BA, QA
Expected vs Actual: Expected: System should show actual available stock
Actual: Item was marked available but wasn't physically present
Impact: Order cancelled, poor customer experience, lost sale

metadata.title: Stock Shows Available but Item is Not Found in Store

text:
Scenario 2: Overstocking at One Store, Stockout at Another
Domain: Replenishment & Demand Planning
Scenario: A product is overstocked in one store with low demand but out of stock in a nearby high-demand store.
What Went Wrong: Demand forecast was applied uniformly without location-specific sales patterns.
Who Was Involved: Inventory Planning Team, Warehouse, Stores, BA, Dev
Expected vs Actual: Expected: Inventory distributed based on demand per location
Actual: Mismatch in supply and demand
Impact: Loss of sales, high holding cost, inter-store transfer overhead

metadata.title: Overstocking at One Store, Stockout at Another

text:
Scenario 3: Automatic Replenishment Didn't Trigger
Domain: Replenishment Logic
Scenario: A fast-selling item reaches below minimum threshold at a store, but no replenishment order is generated.
What Went Wrong: Reorder rule was misconfigured or disabled in the system.
Who Was Involved: Inventory System, Store, Supply Chain Team, BA, QA
Expected vs Actual: Expected: Auto-replenishment should trigger once threshold is hit
Actual: No replenishment; stockout occurred
Impact: Loss of revenue, customer dissatisfaction, manual intervention required

metadata.title: Automatic Replenishment Didn't Trigger

text:
Scenario 4: Vendor Delivered Items Without Barcode Tags
Domain: Vendor Compliance / Receiving
Scenario: A vendor delivers a bulk order, but many items are missing barcodes and SKU tags.
What Went Wrong: Vendor ignored packaging standards; receiving team couldn't scan items into system.
Who Was Involved: Vendor, Warehouse Team, Inventory System, Vendor Management Team
Expected vs Actual: Expected: Items delivered with proper SKU labeling
Actual: Manual work needed to tag and upload SKU data
Impact: Delay in stocking, chances of SKU mismatch, inventory errors

metadata.title: Vendor Delivered Items Without Barcode Tags

text:
Scenario 5: Negative Inventory Value in System
Domain: Inventory Integrity
Scenario: Inventory system shows -2 quantity for a product after multiple returns and cancellations.
What Went Wrong: Inventory updates were not synchronized properly across systems (POS, OMS, Returns Portal).
Who Was Involved: Inventory System, OMS, POS, Return Module, BA, QA, Developer
Expected vs Actual: Expected: Inventory count should never go below 0
Actual: System allowed negative stock due to delayed updates
Impact: Incorrect availability on frontend, false commitments, system confusion

metadata.title: Negative Inventory Value in System

Scenario 1: Wrong Item Picked and Shipped
Domain: Picking Accuracy
Scenario: A customer orders a black wallet, but receives a brown belt instead.
What Went Wrong: Picker selected the wrong item from the shelf due to similar packaging and SKU code mismatch.
Who Was Involved: Customer, Warehouse Picker, WMS, Courier Partner
Expected vs Actual: Expected: Correct item picked, verified, and shipped
Actual: Incorrect item sent, leading to customer return
Impact: Extra return cost, delayed replacement, customer frustration

Domain: Packing / Dispatch
Scenario: An order is packed and ready, but dispatch is delayed by a day because shipping label was not generated.
What Went Wrong: Integration between WMS and courier system failed to auto-generate the label.
Who Was Involved: Warehouse Packer, WMS, Courier API, BA, QA
Expected vs Actual: Expected: Label auto-generated at packing stage
Actual: Manual intervention required to print label
Impact: Shipping delay, missed SLA, increase in customer support tickets

metadata.title: Delay in Dispatch Due to Missing Label

text:
Scenario 3: Item Marked as Shipped But Still in Warehouse
Domain: Order Visibility / Fulfillment Status
Scenario: Customer sees their order as "Shipped" on the website, but it's actually still lying in the warehouse due to pickup delay.
What Went Wrong: WMS status was updated without actual handover to the courier.
Who Was Involved: Warehouse, Courier Partner, OMS, BA, Dev
Expected vs Actual: Expected: Status to update only after package handover
Actual: Shipment status pushed too early
Impact: False tracking info, customer confusion, escalations

metadata.title: Item Marked as Shipped But Still in Warehouse

text:
Scenario 4: Inventory Count Doesn't Match Physical Stock
Domain: Inventory Audit
Scenario: WMS reports 150 units of a product in stock, but during physical audit, only 138 units are found.
What Went Wrong: Improper bin movement tracking and missed barcode scanning during internal transfers.
Who Was Involved: Warehouse Staff, Inventory Team, WMS, Audit Team
Expected vs Actual: Expected: Digital stock count matches physical stock
Actual: Mismatch caused by system gaps
Impact: Reconciliation needed, stock adjustments, order routing errors

metadata.title: Inventory Count Doesn't Match Physical Stock

text:
Scenario 5: Order Lost in Transit After Handover
Domain: Last Mile Delivery
Scenario: Customer's package was handed over to the courier, but no tracking updates appear, and the customer doesn't receive the item.
What Went Wrong: Courier partner failed to scan the package after pickup; no handoff proof exists.
Who Was Involved: Courier Partner, Warehouse, OMS, Customer Support
Expected vs Actual: 🔹 Expected: Tracking to begin immediately after pickup
🔹 Actual: Order marked as picked but vanished in transit
Impact: Reshipment needed, refund processing, customer dissatisfaction

Scenario: A customer uses a coupon that expired yesterday, but the system still applies the discount.

Actual: System incorrectly honored an expired coupon

Expected vs Actual: Expected: Tier should auto-upgrade once threshold is crossed

Impact: Customer dissatisfaction, perceived unfairness
